title: What I think about bubble cities

transcript.text: Hello, this video is what I think about bubble cities in the future.
A lot of people have visions that we're going to live in bubble cities in the future.
Because, well, they have visions of them.
And it also scientifically makes sense because we are destroying, in the process of destroying the ozone layer and the environment.
Every time we send a satellite up in space, we tear a hole in the ozone layer.
The ozone layer is growing at a rapid speed.
We're still using aerosols, which is destroying the ozone layer.
We're polluting the air.
And so it is not at all against logic to imagine that in the future we're all going to have to live in bubble cities to protect our skin.
People in New Zealand are growing fungus all over their eyes.
And they have to go in every six months and get it lasered off.
Because they're in no ozone zone.
Because it's recently started growing.
The Maori never grew fungus on their eyes.
And now everybody's growing fungus on their eyes.
So it is a real thing.
And so, you know, you would think that we would have cities encased in big glass domes to protect us from the UV radiation.
And also, well, so I will explain two visions.
I've had two dreams of bubble cities.
And what I'm thinking is maybe I was visiting worlds, or perhaps parallel worlds, where people were living in bubble cities.
And so what I saw in one bubble city was we had, it was like in a desert area.
So maybe the environment was actually destroyed too, no trees growing.
But you're living in houses, and it was actually, even though it was living in a bubble city, and it was safe to believe that it was very, it wasn't a planet that had gotten messed up.
And it wasn't a natural laser, natural planet.
And perhaps it was a, it wasn't a destroyed planet, though.
Perhaps it was just a, it was Mars.
And it was just a vision of the future of Mars.
And it was just a colony on Mars that was in the process of being terraformed and just wasn't ready to have an atmosphere yet.
Scientists do believe that it would take only about 100 years for us to terraform Mars to be able to have oxygen there.
People have ideas about how to do that.
But, so I do perhaps, I don't know, maybe that's in our future.
Because there was water on Mars, and so we could figure out how to turn that place, that planet into actually a habitable planet with an atmosphere again.
But, so maybe this is a vision of that, because there was red and Earth all around.
But the vision that I had was, and it was a very, very, very, it was a utopian world.
It was a very peaceful, very, very comfortable and pleasant place to visit.
But, um, because it was so, it was so, uh, open, um, society.
Um, it was very, it was different houses, and, but only the houses were open.
Um, the houses did not have roofs on them, because the roof was the bubble.
So it was kind of like anybody could have walked into anybody else's house, pretty much, because there was no roof on the house.
Yet everybody had houses and stairs with multiple levels on the houses.
Um, and, and, you know, there was a door from the main hallway, the main street.
People would walk down, up and down.
And I didn't see cars.
But, um, you open up a, um, you know, you go in your, your gate.
It was basically a gate you open, go in the house.
And, uh, you know, so the upstairs would be kind of like a deck, outdoor deck, and that's where you're, and there were rooms that were private, obviously, where you sleep and stuff.
But, you know, there were, like, the living room and stuff was kind of open.
And, and so then right next to it was, uh, was the next guy's house.
And it wasn't very, um, there weren't very big houses at all.
And, um, it was very, very clean.
It wasn't collecting a whole lot of dust, you know, because I guess because of the little bubble.
But it was a nice place.
And then I had another vision, a dream, of, uh, a society that was living actually underground.
Um, and, but it was also another kind of utopia.
Yeah, yeah, it is utopian, but it was kind of definitely a peaceful society.
Um, and it was kind of, it reminded me of a strip mall, indoor strip mall.
Only people lived in it.
Instead of lining the sides of the strip mall having stores, you actually had people's houses.
Uh, they were, like, apartments in, in walls.
And it was lit up.
And it was, like, a wide street that you walked down.
No cars.
Just walking.
And it was also, in the middle of the street there, you had, uh, I think stores and stuff.
And even on the side.
And there were lots of people.
It was kind of more bustly and more packed with people than the, uh, other house.
Uh, neighborhood I just explained, which was more peaceful.
It was kind of like a neighborhood.
And this was kind of like center.
But anyway, um, I think I'm not the only one, um, having bubble city dreams and visions.
And so I think we are envisioning ourselves in the future on a, perhaps a colonized planet or, uh, a parallel earth.
On a planet where they actually had to do that.
Um, yeah.
And maybe even on this earth we will have bubble cities.
Because there are advantages.
You can control the temperature.
You can, um, oil protect from the sunshine.
Sunlight.
It keeps it clean.
Um, but, um, so I don't know.
I wouldn't like living in one of those.
But that's what I think about bubble cities.
I'm, uh, I'm throwing my two cents in.
